What It's Like to be a Real-Estate Agent to DC's Super Rich | Washingtonian

Washingtonian · by Cathy Alter · April 2, 2017

The bricked-and-columned behemoth at 3017 N Street, Northwest, is nothing close to turnkey. Known as the Kennedy house for the period when the widowed Jackie lived there, it last belonged to her friend Yolande Fox, Miss America 1951, who bought it in 1975. An 8,196-square-foot mansion trapped in amber, it retains a butler\xe2\x80\x99s pantry, a creaky back stairway up to the maid\xe2\x80\x99s quarters, and, according to the sales brochure, a kitchen \xe2\x80\x9crumored to have the same cabinetry and colors that the first lady installed in 1964.\xe2\x80\x9d

Listed last June for $9.95 million, it is an upper fixer-upper. But look past the well-trodden carpeting and scuffed hardwood and there\xe2\x80\x99s a faded glory channeling old Hollywood\xe2\x80\x94walls of celadon silk, murals of midcentury silver-screen stars, a wet bar on the second-floor landing\xe2\x80\x94relics, likely, of Fox\xe2\x80\x99s days as the wife of a Universal Pictures executive. When she died last year, she was remembered as \xe2\x80\x9cthe last of the Georgetown grandes dames.\xe2\x80\x9d

The home is currently in the hands of another grande dame, Washington Fine Properties super-agent Nancy Taylor Bubes. At 62, she rules the high-end markets of Georgetown, Kalorama, and Massachusetts Avenue Heights with a Bluetooth and a Rolodex rivaling that of any of the bigwig lobbyists in town. She\xe2\x80\x99s the top-selling real-estate agent by sales volume in DC. According to the Wall Street Journal/Real Trends, she ranked 34th in the country in 2016, with more than $174 million in sales. Over that year, she sold more than 70 homes, ranging from $365,000 to $9.1 million.

For those who traffic in the tippy-top-dollar properties that might attract a billionaire cabinet secretary, it\xe2\x80\x99s an excellent time to be a real-estate agent.

The Kennedy house, though, has been tough to move. So one day last fall, the price having dropped to $8.995 million and a 3-percent commission of $250,000-plus at stake, Bubes turned on the hustle. At an open house, she sat in the cavernous living room with her aged-to-perfection Louis Vuitton Neverfull.

\xe2\x80\x9cOh my God, oh my God, oh my God,\xe2\x80\x9d she says by way of greeting when her iPhone rings. \xe2\x80\x9cI was just thinking about you!\xe2\x80\x9d

On the other end is Dolly Fox, Yolande\xe2\x80\x99s only child, calling from New York. After issuing a string of four-letter words that would have made Jackie O blush and a mock threat about Fox taking her \xe2\x80\x9ceffing pink diamond ring\xe2\x80\x9d to the grave instead of handing it over to the bill collectors, Bubes determines that Fox is freaking out about how much the upcoming estate sale might net. Fox also reveals she\xe2\x80\x99s coming to Washington soon and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want to spend the night alone in the house.

\xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re too funny! I love it!\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes tells her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ll bring my pillow and we\xe2\x80\x99ll have a slumber party!\xe2\x80\x9d

She hangs up when a guy in sweatpants arrives with someone who either is an agent or is pretending to be one to see where Jackie Kennedy slept. Bubes isn\xe2\x80\x99t quite sure herself but takes them around anyway, making sure they see the cupola and letting them ride the elevator. She\xe2\x80\x99s pretty certain they won\xe2\x80\x99t make an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cBut you never know,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve learned to never underestimate a man in sweatpants.\xe2\x80\x9d

With a steady increase DC home prices over the past seven years, according to Bright MLS, Washington\xe2\x80\x99s multiple listing service, and the arrival of a new administration, it\xe2\x80\x99s a pretty excellent time to be a real-estate agent in Washington. It\xe2\x80\x99s even more excellent if you traffic in the tippy-top-dollar properties with infinity pools and internet-of-things automation that might attract a newly confirmed billionaire Cabinet secretary or other deep-pocketed transplants.

These are Nancy Taylor Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s people. Which is sort of remarkable, considering that Bubes is the most un-Washington of Washingtonians. Totally unedited, resembling a more Herm\xc3\xa8s version of Kathie Lee Gifford, Bubes has a la-di-dah air that\xe2\x80\x99s at odds with the popular notion of high-end agents as chilly sophisticates or imperious majordomos\xe2\x80\x94and even more at odds with the serious-minded bigwigs who seem to have her on speed dial. Yet her loopy vibe is a magnet. As Capitol Hill agent Tom Faison, a 25-year industry veteran, puts it: \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s zany like a fox.\xe2\x80\x9d

And as word of one satisfied customer makes its way around the village, one R Street rowhouse bought turns into another Water Street condo sold. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s so simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Faison says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe made one deal and did it well. Those people broadcast their good deal because everybody loves to talk about real estate at any dinner party in Georgetown.\xe2\x80\x9d

Bubes did a deal at a party once. Her friend Melissa Overmyer met a prospective buyer for the P Street house she owned with her husband, Dale, an architect, at one Georgetown soir\xc3\xa9e. Bubes was at another. \xe2\x80\x9cI start getting one text after another from Melissa, saying she has sold her house and that they are agreeing to all the terms on a cocktail napkin,\xe2\x80\x9d when in comes a photo of Melissa and the new buyer with said napkin, which read, \xe2\x80\x9c$2.9MM . . . No contingency, cash offer.\xe2\x80\x9d

The following day, Dale Overmyer remembers, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy said, \xe2\x80\x98You are going to honor your cocktail napkin. A deal\xe2\x80\x99s a deal.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Walk into the George Town Club with Bubes and she\xe2\x80\x99s helloing all over the room. Every July, she and her seven-person staff plant 1,600 American flags in Georgetown front yards, her business card attached. Every Halloween, her corner-lot Victorian turns into a haunted house with animatronic mummies and monsters, a creepy fortuneteller right out of a penny arcade, and ghostly noises emanating from the bushes. The whole neighborhood is there rubbernecking while Bubes, her ginger-blond hair poking out of an oversize witch\xe2\x80\x99s hat, mans a popcorn station or hands out candy bars.

Bubes has occupied the 5,000-square-footer at 31st and Q for 18 years, only the second she\xe2\x80\x99s owned in the neighborhood. (She also has homes in Palm Beach and Rehoboth.) \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m a Taurus,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI get very settled.\xe2\x80\x9d But she\xe2\x80\x99ll know before you do when you\xe2\x80\x99re ready for a refresh and what might suit.

\xe2\x80\x9cShe knows everybody in town and every house in town,\xe2\x80\x9d says Mary Louise Kelly, a novelist and NPR\xe2\x80\x99s national-security correspondent. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen one comes open, she\xe2\x80\x99ll run into someone she knows and say, \xe2\x80\x98Maybe you should think about moving.\xe2\x80\x99 Suddenly in a week, you\xe2\x80\x99re buying a house.\xe2\x80\x9d

Kelly and her husband did. They were living in Italy temporarily, not really looking to leave their permanent residence on O Street, but suddenly a gray Victorian on Q with a white lattice portico and that rarest of finds in Georgetown\xe2\x80\x94parking\xe2\x80\x94appeared. They saw the house on a quick trip to DC, had no financing in place, made an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cMy husband, Nick, was on crutches and couldn\xe2\x80\x99t walk,\xe2\x80\x9d says Kelly. \xe2\x80\x9cHe was literally crawling up and down the stairs on all fours.\xe2\x80\x9d

Bubes had no trouble unloading the O Street property they left behind. She knew a couple on R who wanted to downsize.

Bubes hails from Fredericksburg, Virginia, where her father, Clyde, owned an auto-parts store and her mother, Mabel, played piano in the Baptist church. One day, for my benefit, she calls her cousin Norman from her Champagne-colored 740-series BMW and puts him on speaker. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, doll baby,\xe2\x80\x9d he says in the family\xe2\x80\x99s Dukes of Hazzard accent that Bubes has worked hard to lose. \xe2\x80\x9cHow you doing with those crooks up in Washington?\xe2\x80\x9d

Bubes graduated from East Carolina University with a degree in fine art. But \xe2\x80\x9cI was not going to be selling pots for a living,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. She moved to Washington hoping to work at the Smithsonian, but no positions were open, so she began selling designer clothing at Neiman Marcus. At 29, after a transfer to the in-house PR department, Bubes was fired, she says, for eating a piece of chocolate while working a party. (The help was not supposed to help themselves.)

5 of the Priciest Homes Bubes Has Sold in the Past Year

1. $9.1 million, 3134 Ellicott St., NW
2. $4.7 million, 3600 Ordway St., NW
3. $4.4 million, 3310 P St., NW
4. $3.76 million, 4431 Klingle St., NW
5. $3.73 million, 1417 33rd St., NW

Bubes had enjoyed sales and wanted to try it on a larger scale. She got her real-estate license and began working at Dale Denton. While there, she bought a one-bedroom condo in DC\xe2\x80\x99s McLean Gardens for $47,000. She decided to target her neighbors in the complex. Her average sale was $200,000. She went to Century 21, then Pardoe and Coldwell Banker, then Washington Fine Properties. \xe2\x80\x9cSome agents get big eyes for big properties,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI could sell five houses in the time it took them to sell one.\xe2\x80\x9d

When Bubes was 35, she married and moved to Georgetown. At first, she didn\xe2\x80\x99t see a way to compete with its established high-end agents. But having kids, as she did by then, provided a way into the clubby society. She started selling the \xe2\x80\x9clittle stuff,\xe2\x80\x9d such as the tiny rowhouses on 27th Street. From there, she got involved in neighborhood groups and met \xe2\x80\x9clayer upon layer of people.\xe2\x80\x9d By her mid-forties, which coincided with the market boom of the early 2000s, Bubes began to dominate DC. Her sales went from $55 million in 2002 to $100 million just three years later.

\xe2\x80\x9cWhen everyone was selling $1-million-to-$2-million homes, Nancy sold $500,000-to-$600,000 houses and sold lots of them,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan Bubes, her husband of 28 years. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen the market moved to $3 million to $4 million, she was doing $1 million to $2 million. She did a great job of staying just under what everyone else in the market was doing.\xe2\x80\x9d

Bubes used to market herself on the front of Safeway grocery carts. Then Trish Yan, her assistant from 2002 to 2009, helped engineer a Don Draper\xe2\x80\x93style rebranding that cast Bubes as the aspirational ideal through an ad that ran in local glossies. Lounging poolside in the ad were two women, one of whom looked like Bubes. \xe2\x80\x9cThe headline was DISCOVER A WASHINGTON WAY OF LIFE,\xe2\x80\x9d says Yan.

Bubes had moved into a new league. \xe2\x80\x9cThe first time we filed a tax return, my mouth dropped,\xe2\x80\x9d Alan says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe is always out-earning me.\xe2\x80\x9d Alan was until recently CEO of Linens of the Week, a major supplier to the food-service industry.

The couple met early in Nancy\xe2\x80\x99s career when she showed Alan\xe2\x80\x99s mother a property on Grace Street in Georgetown. Bubes m\xc3\xa8re didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the place. But she said to her son, \xe2\x80\x9cShow her your apartment and have her sell that!\xe2\x80\x9d

After dating four years, Alan and Nancy eloped to Lake Tahoe. Close friend Maury Povich, the TV personality, whom Alan met when he was 16 and caddied for at Woodmont Country Club, was the only witness. Instead of a wedding album, the Bubeses have a video of Povich spoofing his hosting duties on A Current Affair.

The couple is so close to Povich and his wife, Connie Chung, that the latter are godparents to one of the Bubeses\xe2\x80\x99 three children. The foursome take vacations together twice a year\xe2\x80\x94the rare instance, Chung says, when Bubes can survive with no plans or schedule: \xe2\x80\x9cWe just become idiots and let our brains rest.\xe2\x80\x9d Otherwise, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy is relentless in her work.\xe2\x80\x9d

She \xe2\x80\x9calways has to be busy,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan. \xe2\x80\x9cIf she\xe2\x80\x99s on the phone, she\xe2\x80\x99s on her stationary bike or needlepointing belt after belt. She did sell a house when she was in labor with our first child.\xe2\x80\x9d

Says Nancy: \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t have an assistant back then!\xe2\x80\x9d

Watching Bubes in action is like watching one of those Hindu deities with all the arms. Take the splashy preview party she hosted at the Massachusetts Avenue Heights home of the late financier and venture capitalist Melvyn Estrin. Looking like the offspring of Versailles and the Parthenon, the mansion had two life-size sculptures of horses prancing on the front lawn, neoclassical statues all over the marble foyer, and carpets so pristine that everyone was given surgical slippers before being allowed upstairs, where Estrin\xe2\x80\x99s widow received guests while perched on a settee in the master suite\xe2\x80\x99s sitting room.

Throughout the affair, Bubes stood under a skylight rotunda, somehow managing to balance a plate of hors d\xe2\x80\x99oeuvres, hug and kiss almost everyone who walked in\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cOkay, you\xe2\x80\x99re going to die\xe2\x80\x9d is her hello\xe2\x80\x94and pass out swag bags. The house\xe2\x80\x99s sale price two weeks later: $12.25 million.

\xe2\x80\x9cNancy is a \xe2\x80\x98periodic,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d explains Doug Komlenic, her business coach. \xe2\x80\x9cShe can engage me and you and engage in a multiple of other tasks and be present and in the moment for each one\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94a true multitasker. Komlenic, who\xe2\x80\x99s based in San Diego, talks to Bubes twice a month. He discourages other clients from checking e-mail or reading their newsfeeds, but not Nancy. Of 83 clients, she\xe2\x80\x99s one of only two periodics\xe2\x80\x94a human Lamborghini, prone to leaving those who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep up in the dust. \xe2\x80\x9cOur challenge from day one has been managing her team,\xe2\x80\x9d Komlenic says. \xe2\x80\x9cWe are looking for someone to be more of a leader so Nancy can delegate and then that person can delegate.\xe2\x80\x9d

she used to market herself on safeway grocery carts. then things changed.

Yan, who used to be that person, remembers doing juice cleanses with Bubes and being such intimates that they could finish each other\xe2\x80\x99s sentences. Not that working for Bubes was all sleepovers and hair braiding: \xe2\x80\x9cThe phone would ring at all hours or Nancy would knock on my door at 7 am and say, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99m just walking by.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d While in Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s employ, Yan rented a house on 33rd Street that Bubes owned. The arrangement contributed to their eventual breakup, after Yan came to believe that Bubes had outright purchased a home for the family nanny. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99ve grown someone to $100 million and I\xe2\x80\x99m still renting?\xe2\x80\x9d Yan asks reflectively. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s a huge disconnect.\xe2\x80\x9d

Says Bubes: \xe2\x80\x9cI feel badly that she felt this way, because we were very, very close.\xe2\x80\x9d

Cara McClure, who\xe2\x80\x99s now a real-estate agent in Palm Beach, enjoyed the not-in-the-job-description duties Bubes assigned her during her 18 months with the firm, such as caddying for Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son. \xe2\x80\x9cI remember thinking, \xe2\x80\x98I get to have fun at this golf course and get a good workout at the same time!\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Less fun was the night McClure stayed late to help Bubes with her taxes. \xe2\x80\x9cBut Nancy brought me home-cooked salmon and sat there with me the whole time,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99ll be in the trenches with you.\xe2\x80\x9d

Bubes takes on everyone as family, according to McClure. In fact, she did buy a place to house the nanny\xe2\x80\x94but it was not a gift. She charged her rent, just as she did Yan. The home was close to Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son\xe2\x80\x99s school, so the nanny could take over carpooling duties. Bubes was too afraid to chauffeur the boy herself, the result of a highway mishap seven years ago. She was driving back to DC from Fredericksburg and ran out of gas, right in the middle lane of I-395. (A good Samaritan pulled over and walked across three lanes of traffic to rescue her.)

A conflicted relationship with the automobile is yet another reason why Bubes has integrated so well into car-averse Georgetown. Also why she has a very good therapist. \xe2\x80\x9cShe came to one of my open houses,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says, cracking up. \xe2\x80\x9cWe started talking, and she says that she\xe2\x80\x99s a behavioral therapist who specializes in phobias and I said, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, my God, do I need you.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Yes, Bubes sold her shrink a house.

A real-estate agent who\xe2\x80\x99s leery of driving seems like the ultimate paradox, but within the roughly two-square-mile province where Bubes reigns, it squares perfectly. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m not driving 30 minutes to show something in Virginia,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m running five blocks.\xe2\x80\x9d Running those blocks at Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s side is like trying to keep up with a mayor. She stops to talk to absolutely everyone, using first names and asking after the whole brood\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s all about dogs and kids,\xe2\x80\x9d she tells me\xe2\x80\x94or inquiring about an upcoming fundraiser of which she herself is usually a chair, donor, or board member. Bubes belongs to Adas Israel, having converted from Protestantism to Judaism after she married. She also attends 7:30 Mass at Epiphany Catholic Church every morning. She has her bases covered.

\xe2\x80\x9cI did that sale,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, pointing to the notorious Drath house, where in 2011 47-year-old Albrecht Muth murdered Viola Drath, his 91-year-old wife. There\xe2\x80\x99s a house on P Street that she\xe2\x80\x99s sold four times since 1997.

The one-percenter buyers of today\xe2\x80\x99s Washington want clean lines, contemporary interiors, \xe2\x80\x9cbigger closets, bigger bathrooms, and en-suite master bathrooms with a double sink,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says. Also: \xe2\x80\x9cThey all want elevators.\xe2\x80\x9d She stages her clients\xe2\x80\x99 homes in the full spectrum of neutrals and advises them to paint the walls white. \xe2\x80\x9cTwenty years ago,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, \xe2\x80\x9cyou had more people who would . . . replicate the Victorian style\xe2\x80\x94interiors with dark wood, heavy drapery.\xe2\x80\x9d Back then, she recalls fondly, \xe2\x80\x9cpeople paid extra for those sloping floors.\xe2\x80\x9d

Not that she\xe2\x80\x99s nostalgic. There\xe2\x80\x99s always a deep-pocketed developer (Douglas Jemal) or attorney (\xe2\x80\x9cDC Hammer\xe2\x80\x9d Mike Slocumb) among her past clients who might call up in search of a change of scenery. Her VIPs are one subject on which Bubes is remarkably tight-lipped. Asked about a rumor that she recently took a Middle Eastern royal house-hunting, she declines to comment. What about whispers of her having hung out at the Trump Hotel on Christmas Eve\xe2\x80\x94and not just because she likes the lobby Ivanka Trump designed? Another no-comment.

Bubes does credit the Trump White House for a recent upsurge in business. \xe2\x80\x9cIn my 32 years of being in real estate, this is the first time we\xe2\x80\x99ve had an administration come in that\xe2\x80\x99s buying and not renting,\xe2\x80\x9d she says.

As of this writing, Bubes has ten listings totaling about $50 million. The Kennedy house is still among them. It\xe2\x80\x99s been reduced again, to $6.5 million. But owner Dolly Fox isn\xe2\x80\x99t worried. Bubes has sold property for her before. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve dealt with a lot of realtors,\xe2\x80\x9d Fox says, \xe2\x80\x9cand it\xe2\x80\x99s always Nancy who comes in at the last minute and sells.\xe2\x80\x9d

Photographs by Marquis Perkins.

This article appears in the April 2017 issue of Washingtonian.

50,000- plus at stake, Bubes turned on the hustle. At an open house, she sat in the cavernous living room with her aged- to- perfection Louis Vuitton Neverfull. \xe2\x80\x9cOh my God, oh my God, oh my God,\xe2\x80\x9d she says by way of greeting when her iPhone rings. \xe2\x80\x9cI was just thinking about you!\xe2\x80\x9d On the other end is Dolly Fox , Yolande\xe2\x80\x99s only child, calling from New York. After issuing a string of four- letter words that would have made Jackie O blush and a mock threat about Fox taking her \xe2\x80\x9ceffing pink diamond ring\xe2\x80\x9d to the grave instead of handing it over to the bill collectors, Bubes determines that Fox is freaking out about how much the upcoming estate sale might net. Fox also reveals she\xe2\x80\x99s coming to Washington soon and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want to spend the night alone in the house. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re too funny! I love it!\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes tells her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ll bring my pillow and we\xe2\x80\x99ll have a slumber party!\xe2\x80\x9d She hangs up when a guy in sweatpants arrives with someone who either is an agent or is pretending to be one to see where Jackie Kennedy slept. Bubes isn\xe2\x80\x99t quite sure herself but takes them around anyway, making sure they see the cupola and letting them ride the elevator. She\xe2\x80\x99s pretty certain they won\xe2\x80\x99t make an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cBut you never know,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve learned to never underestimate a man in sweatpants.\xe2\x80\x9d With a steady increase DC home prices over the past seven years, according to Bright MLS, Washington\xe2\x80\x99s multiple listing service, and the arrival of a new administration, it\xe2\x80\x99s a pretty excellent time to be a real- estate agent in Washington. It\xe2\x80\x99s even more excellent if you traffic in the tippy- top- dollar properties with infinity pools and internet- of- things automation that might attract a newly confirmed billionaire Cabinet secretary or other deep- pocketed transplants. These are Nancy Taylor Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s people. Which is sort of remarkable, considering that Bubes is the most un- Washington of Washingtonians. Totally unedited, resembling a more Herm\xc3\xa8s version of Kathie Lee Gifford, Bubes has a la- di- dah air that\xe2\x80\x99s at odds with the popular notion of high- end agents as chilly sophisticates or imperious majordomos\xe2\x80\x94 and even more at odds with the serious- minded bigwigs who seem to have her on speed dial. Yet her loopy vibe is a magnet. As Capitol Hill agent Tom Faison, a 25- year industry veteran, puts it: \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s zany like a fox.\xe2\x80\x9d And as word of one satisfied customer makes its way around the village, one R Street rowhouse bought turns into another Water Street condo sold. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s so simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Faison says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe made one deal and did it well. Those people broadcast their good deal because everybody loves to talk about real estate at any dinner party in Georgetown.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes did a deal at a party once. Her friend Melissa Overmyer met a prospective buyer for the P Street house she owned with her husband, Dale, an architect, at one Georgetown soir\xc3\xa9e. Bubes was at another. \xe2\x80\x9cI start getting one text after another from Melissa, saying she has sold her house and that they are agreeing to all the terms on a cocktail napkin,\xe2\x80\x9d when in comes a photo of Melissa and the new buyer with said napkin, which read, \xe2\x80\x9c$2.9MM . . . No contingency, cash offer.\xe2\x80\x9d The following day, Dale Overmyer remembers, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy said, \xe2\x80\x98You are going to honor your cocktail napkin. A deal\xe2\x80\x99s a deal.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Bubes had the listing for the 8,200- square- foot \xe2\x80\x9cKennedy House,\xe2\x80\x9d where Jackie lived after JFK\xe2\x80\x99s assassination. Walk into the George Town Club with Bubes and she\xe2\x80\x99s helloing all over the room. Every July, she and her seven- person staff plant 1,600 American flags in Georgetown front yards, her business card attached. Every Halloween, her corner- lot Victorian turns into a haunted house with animatronic mummies and monsters, a creepy fortuneteller right out of a penny arcade, and ghostly noises emanating from the bushes. The whole neighborhood is there rubbernecking while Bubes, her ginger- blond hair poking out of an oversize witch\xe2\x80\x99s hat, mans a popcorn station or hands out candy bars. Bubes has occupied the 5,000- square- footer at 31st and Q for 18 years, only the second she\xe2\x80\x99s owned in the neighborhood. (She also has homes in Palm Beach and Rehoboth.) \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m a Taurus,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI get very settled.\xe2\x80\x9d But she\xe2\x80\x99ll know before you do when you\xe2\x80\x99re ready for a refresh and what might suit. \xe2\x80\x9cShe knows everybody in town and every house in town,\xe2\x80\x9d says Mary Louise Kelly , a novelist and NPR\xe2\x80\x99s national- security correspondent. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen one comes open, she\xe2\x80\x99ll run into someone she knows and say, \xe2\x80\x98Maybe you should think about moving.\xe2\x80\x99 Suddenly in a week, you\xe2\x80\x99re buying a house.\xe2\x80\x9d Kelly and her husband did. They were living in Italy temporarily, not really looking to leave their permanent residence on O Street, but suddenly a gray Victorian on Q with a white lattice portico and that rarest of finds in Georgetown\xe2\x80\x94 parking\xe2\x80\x94 appeared. They saw the house on a quick trip to DC, had no financing in place, made an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cMy husband, Nick, was on crutches and couldn\xe2\x80\x99t walk,\xe2\x80\x9d says Kelly. \xe2\x80\x9cHe was literally crawling up and down the stairs on all fours.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes had no trouble unloading the O Street property they left behind. She knew a couple on R who wanted to downsize. Bubes hails from Fredericksburg, Virginia, where her father, Clyde, owned an auto- parts store and her mother, Mabel, played piano in the Baptist church. One day, for my benefit, she calls her cousin Norman from her Champagne- colored 740- series BMW and puts him on speaker. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, doll baby,\xe2\x80\x9d he says in the family\xe2\x80\x99s Dukes of Hazzard accent that Bubes has worked hard to lose. \xe2\x80\x9cHow you doing with those crooks up in Washington?\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes graduated from East Carolina University with a degree in fine art. But \xe2\x80\x9cI was not going to be selling pots for a living,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. She moved to Washington hoping to work at the Smithsonian, but no positions were open, so she began selling designer clothing at Neiman Marcus. At 29, after a transfer to the in- house PR department, Bubes was fired, she says, for eating a piece of chocolate while working a party. (The help was not supposed to help themselves.) 5 of the Priciest Homes Bubes Has Sold in the Past Year Photographs courtesy of Nancy Taylor Bubes. 1. $9.1 million , 3134 Ellicott St., NW 2. $4.7 million , 3600 Ordway St., NW 3. $4.4 million , 3310 P St., NW 4. $3.76 million , 4431 Klingle St., NW 5. $3.73 million , 1417 33rd St., NW Bubes had enjoyed sales and wanted to try it on a larger scale. She got her real- estate license and began working at Dale Denton. While there, she bought a one- bedroom condo in DC\xe2\x80\x99s McLean Gardens for $47,000. She decided to target her neighbors in the complex. Her average sale was $200,000. She went to Century 21, then Pardoe and Coldwell Banker, then Washington Fine Properties. \xe2\x80\x9cSome agents get big eyes for big properties,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI could sell five houses in the time it took them to sell one.\xe2\x80\x9d When Bubes was 35, she married and moved to Georgetown. At first, she didn\xe2\x80\x99t see a way to compete with its established high- end agents. But having kids, as she did by then, provided a way into the clubby society. She started selling the \xe2\x80\x9clittle stuff,\xe2\x80\x9d such as the tiny rowhouses on 27th Street. From there, she got involved in neighborhood groups and met \xe2\x80\x9clayer upon layer of people.\xe2\x80\x9d By her mid- forties, which coincided with the market boom of the early 2000s, Bubes began to dominate DC. Her sales went from $55 million in 2002 to $100 million just three years later. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen everyone was selling $1- million- to- $2- million homes, Nancy sold $500,000- to- $600,000 houses and sold lots of them,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan Bubes, her husband of 28 years. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen the market moved to $3 million to $4 million, she was doing $1 million to $2 million. She did a great job of staying just under what everyone else in the market was doing.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes used to market herself on the front of Safeway grocery carts. Then Trish Yan , her assistant from 2002 to 2009, helped engineer a Don Draper\xe2\x80\x93 style rebranding that cast Bubes as the aspirational ideal through an ad that ran in local glossies. Lounging poolside in the ad were two women, one of whom looked like Bubes. \xe2\x80\x9cThe headline was DISCOVER A WASHINGTON WAY OF LIFE,\xe2\x80\x9d says Yan. Bubes had moved into a new league. \xe2\x80\x9cThe first time we filed a tax return, my mouth dropped,\xe2\x80\x9d Alan says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe is always out- earning me.\xe2\x80\x9d Alan was until recently CEO of Linens of the Week, a major supplier to the food- service industry. The couple met early in Nancy\xe2\x80\x99s career when she showed Alan\xe2\x80\x99s mother a property on Grace Street in Georgetown. Bubes m\xc3\xa8re didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the place. But she said to her son, \xe2\x80\x9cShow her your apartment and have her sell that!\xe2\x80\x9d After dating four years, Alan and Nancy eloped to Lake Tahoe. Close friend Maury Povich , the TV personality, whom Alan met when he was 16 and caddied for at Woodmont Country Club, was the only witness. Instead of a wedding album, the Bubeses have a video of Povich spoofing his hosting duties on A Current Affair . The couple is so close to Povich and his wife, Connie Chung , that the latter are godparents to one of the Bubeses\xe2\x80\x99 three children. The foursome take vacations together twice a year\xe2\x80\x94 the rare instance, Chung says, when Bubes can survive with no plans or schedule: \xe2\x80\x9cWe just become idiots and let our brains rest.\xe2\x80\x9d Otherwise, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy is relentless in her work.\xe2\x80\x9d She \xe2\x80\x9calways has to be busy,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan. \xe2\x80\x9cIf she\xe2\x80\x99s on the phone, she\xe2\x80\x99s on her stationary bike or needlepointing belt after belt. She did sell a house when she was in labor with our first child.\xe2\x80\x9d Says Nancy: \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t have an assistant back then!\xe2\x80\x9d Watching Bubes in action is like watching one of those Hindu deities with all the arms. Take the splashy preview party she hosted at the Massachusetts Avenue Heights home of the late financier and venture capitalist Melvyn Estrin . Looking like the offspring of Versailles and the Parthenon, the mansion had two life- size sculptures of horses prancing on the front lawn, neoclassical statues all over the marble foyer, and carpets so pristine that everyone was given surgical slippers before being allowed upstairs, where Estrin\xe2\x80\x99s widow received guests while perched on a settee in the master suite\xe2\x80\x99s sitting room. Throughout the affair, Bubes stood under a skylight rotunda, somehow managing to balance a plate of hors d\xe2\x80\x99oeuvres, hug and kiss almost everyone who walked in\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cOkay, you\xe2\x80\x99re going to die\xe2\x80\x9d is her hello\xe2\x80\x94 and pass out swag bags. The house\xe2\x80\x99s sale price two weeks later: $12.25 million. \xe2\x80\x9cNancy is a \xe2\x80\x98periodic,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d explains Doug Komlenic, her business coach. \xe2\x80\x9cShe can engage me and you and engage in a multiple of other tasks and be present and in the moment for each one\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 a true multitasker. Komlenic, who\xe2\x80\x99s based in San Diego, talks to Bubes twice a month. He discourages other clients from checking e- mail or reading their newsfeeds, but not Nancy. Of 83 clients, she\xe2\x80\x99s one of only two periodics\xe2\x80\x94 a human Lamborghini, prone to leaving those who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep up in the dust. \xe2\x80\x9cOur challenge from day one has been managing her team,\xe2\x80\x9d Komlenic says. \xe2\x80\x9cWe are looking for someone to be more of a leader so Nancy can delegate and then that person can delegate.\xe2\x80\x9d she used to market herself on safeway grocery carts. then things changed. Yan, who used to be that person, remembers doing juice cleanses with Bubes and being such intimates that they could finish each other\xe2\x80\x99s sentences. Not that working for Bubes was all sleepovers and hair braiding: \xe2\x80\x9cThe phone would ring at all hours or Nancy would knock on my door at 7 am and say, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99m just walking by.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d While in Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s employ, Yan rented a house on 33rd Street that Bubes owned. The arrangement contributed to their eventual breakup, after Yan came to believe that Bubes had outright purchased a home for the family nanny. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99ve grown someone to $100 million and I\xe2\x80\x99m still renting?\xe2\x80\x9d Yan asks reflectively. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s a huge disconnect.\xe2\x80\x9d Says Bubes: \xe2\x80\x9cI feel badly that she felt this way, because we were very, very close.\xe2\x80\x9d Cara McClure , who\xe2\x80\x99s now a real- estate agent in Palm Beach, enjoyed the not- in- the- job- description duties Bubes assigned her during her 18 months with the firm, such as caddying for Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son. \xe2\x80\x9cI remember thinking, \xe2\x80\x98I get to have fun at this golf course and get a good workout at the same time!\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Less fun was the night McClure stayed late to help Bubes with her taxes. \xe2\x80\x9cBut Nancy brought me home- cooked salmon and sat there with me the whole time,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99ll be in the trenches with you.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes takes on everyone as family, according to McClure. In fact, she did buy a place to house the nanny\xe2\x80\x94 but it was not a gift. She charged her rent, just as she did Yan. The home was close to Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son\xe2\x80\x99s school, so the nanny could take over carpooling duties. Bubes was too afraid to chauffeur the boy herself, the result of a highway mishap seven years ago. She was driving back to DC from Fredericksburg and ran out of gas, right in the middle lane of I- 395. (A good Samaritan pulled over and walked across three lanes of traffic to rescue her.) A conflicted relationship with the automobile is yet another reason why Bubes has integrated so well into car- averse Georgetown. Also why she has a very good therapist. \xe2\x80\x9cShe came to one of my open houses,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says, cracking up. \xe2\x80\x9cWe started talking, and she says that she\xe2\x80\x99s a behavioral therapist who specializes in phobias and I said, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, my God, do I need you.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Yes, Bubes sold her shrink a house. A real- estate agent who\xe2\x80\x99s leery of driving seems like the ultimate paradox, but within the roughly two- square- mile province where Bubes reigns, it squares perfectly. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m not driving 30 minutes to show something in Virginia,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m running five blocks.\xe2\x80\x9d Running those blocks at Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s side is like trying to keep up with a mayor. She stops to talk to absolutely everyone, using first names and asking after the whole brood\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s all about dogs and kids,\xe2\x80\x9d she tells me\xe2\x80\x94 or inquiring about an upcoming fundraiser of which she herself is usually a chair, donor, or board member. Bubes belongs to Adas Israel, having converted from Protestantism to Judaism after she married. She also attends 7:30 Mass at Epiphany Catholic Church every morning. She has her bases covered. \xe2\x80\x9cI did that sale,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, pointing to the notorious Drath house, where in 2011 47- year- old Albrecht Muth murdered Viola Drath, his 91- year- old wife. There\xe2\x80\x99s a house on P Street that she\xe2\x80\x99s sold four times since 1997. The one- percenter buyers of today\xe2\x80\x99s Washington want clean lines, contemporary interiors, \xe2\x80\x9cbigger closets, bigger bathrooms, and en- suite master bathrooms with a double sink,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says. Also: \xe2\x80\x9cThey all want elevators.\xe2\x80\x9d She stages her clients\xe2\x80\x99 homes in the full spectrum of neutrals and advises them to paint the walls white. \xe2\x80\x9cTwenty years ago,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, \xe2\x80\x9cyou had more people who would . . . replicate the Victorian style\xe2\x80\x94 interiors with dark wood, heavy drapery.\xe2\x80\x9d Back then, she recalls fondly, \xe2\x80\x9cpeople paid extra for those sloping floors.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9c$2.9MM . . . No contingency, cash offer\xe2\x80\x9d: a deal done on a cocktail napkin. Bubes was at one Georgetown party; her seller and buyer wrote out the terms at another. Not that she\xe2\x80\x99s nostalgic. There\xe2\x80\x99s always a deep- pocketed developer (Douglas Jemal) or attorney (\xe2\x80\x9cDC Hammer\xe2\x80\x9d Mike Slocumb) among her past clients who might call up in search of a change of scenery. Her VIPs are one subject on which Bubes is remarkably tight- lipped. Asked about a rumor that she recently took a Middle Eastern royal house- hunting, she declines to comment. What about whispers of her having hung out at the Trump Hotel on Christmas Eve\xe2\x80\x94 and not just because she likes the lobby Ivanka Trump designed? Another no- comment. Bubes does credit the Trump White House for a recent upsurge in business. \xe2\x80\x9cIn my 32 years of being in real estate, this is the first time we\xe2\x80\x99ve had an administration come in that\xe2\x80\x99s buying and not renting,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. As of this writing, Bubes has ten listings totaling about $50 million. The Kennedy house is still among them. It\xe2\x80\x99s been reduced again, to $6.5 million. But owner Dolly Fox isn\xe2\x80\x99t worried. Bubes has sold property for her before. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve dealt with a lot of realtors,\xe2\x80\x9d Fox says, \xe2\x80\x9cand it\xe2\x80\x99s always Nancy who comes in at the last minute and sells.\xe2\x80\x9d Photographs by Marquis Perkins. This article appears in the April 2017 issue of Washingtonian. Share this story! Cathy Alter The bricked- and- columned behemoth at 3017 N Street, Northwest, is nothing close to turnkey. Known as the Kennedy house for the period when the widowed Jackie lived there, it last belonged to her friend Yolande Fox , Miss America 1951, who bought it in 1975. An 8,196- square- foot mansion trapped in amber, it retains a butler\xe2\x80\x99s pantry, a creaky back stairway up to the maid\xe2\x80\x99s quarters, and, according to the sales brochure, a kitchen \xe2\x80\x9crumored to have the same cabinetry and colors that the first lady installed in 1964.\xe2\x80\x9d Listed last June for $9.95 million, it is an upper fixer- upper. But look past the well- trodden carpeting and scuffed hardwood and there\xe2\x80\x99s a faded glory channeling old Hollywood\xe2\x80\x94 walls of celadon silk, murals of midcentury silver- screen stars, a wet bar on the second- floor landing\xe2\x80\x94 relics, likely, of Fox\xe2\x80\x99s days as the wife of a Universal Pictures executive. When she died last year, she was remembered as \xe2\x80\x9cthe last of the Georgetown grandes dames.\xe2\x80\x9d The home is currently in the hands of another grande dame, Washington Fine Properties super- agent Nancy Taylor Bubes . At 62, she rules the high- end markets of Georgetown, Kalorama, and Massachusetts Avenue Heights with a Bluetooth and a Rolodex rivaling that of any of the bigwig lobbyists in town. She\xe2\x80\x99s the top- selling real- estate agent by sales volume in DC. According to the Wall Street Journal Real Trends, she ranked 34th in the country in 2016, with more than $174 million in sales. Over that year, she sold more than 70 homes, ranging from $365,000 to $9.1 million. For those who traffic in the tippy- top- dollar properties that might attract a billionaire cabinet secretary, it\xe2\x80\x99s an excellent time to be a real- estate agent. The Kennedy house, though, has been tough to move. So one day last fall, the price having dropped to $8.995 million and a 3- percent commission of $250,000- plus at stake, Bubes turned on the hustle. At an open house, she sat in the cavernous living room with her aged- to- perfection Louis Vuitton Neverfull. \xe2\x80\x9cOh my God, oh my God, oh my God,\xe2\x80\x9d she says by way of greeting when her iPhone rings. \xe2\x80\x9cI was just thinking about you!\xe2\x80\x9d On the other end is Dolly Fox , Yolande\xe2\x80\x99s only child, calling from New York. After issuing a string of four- letter words that would have made Jackie O blush and a mock threat about Fox taking her \xe2\x80\x9ceffing pink diamond ring\xe2\x80\x9d to the grave instead of handing it over to the bill collectors, Bubes determines that Fox is freaking out about how much the upcoming estate sale might net. Fox also reveals she\xe2\x80\x99s coming to Washington soon and doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want to spend the night alone in the house. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re too funny! I love it!\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes tells her. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ll bring my pillow and we\xe2\x80\x99ll have a slumber party!\xe2\x80\x9d She hangs up when a guy in sweatpants arrives with someone who either is an agent or is pretending to be one to see where Jackie Kennedy slept. Bubes isn\xe2\x80\x99t quite sure herself but takes them around anyway, making sure they see the cupola and letting them ride the elevator. She\xe2\x80\x99s pretty certain they won\xe2\x80\x99t make an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cBut you never know,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve learned to never underestimate a man in sweatpants.\xe2\x80\x9d With a steady increase DC home prices over the past seven years, according to Bright MLS, Washington\xe2\x80\x99s multiple listing service, and the arrival of a new administration, it\xe2\x80\x99s a pretty excellent time to be a real- estate agent in Washington. It\xe2\x80\x99s even more excellent if you traffic in the tippy- top- dollar properties with infinity pools and internet- of- things automation that might attract a newly confirmed billionaire Cabinet secretary or other deep- pocketed transplants. These are Nancy Taylor Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s people. Which is sort of remarkable, considering that Bubes is the most un- Washington of Washingtonians. Totally unedited, resembling a more Herm\xc3\xa8s version of Kathie Lee Gifford, Bubes has a la- di- dah air that\xe2\x80\x99s at odds with the popular notion of high- end agents as chilly sophisticates or imperious majordomos\xe2\x80\x94 and even more at odds with the serious- minded bigwigs who seem to have her on speed dial. Yet her loopy vibe is a magnet. As Capitol Hill agent Tom Faison, a 25- year industry veteran, puts it: \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s zany like a fox.\xe2\x80\x9d And as word of one satisfied customer makes its way around the village, one R Street rowhouse bought turns into another Water Street condo sold. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s so simple,\xe2\x80\x9d Faison says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe made one deal and did it well. Those people broadcast their good deal because everybody loves to talk about real estate at any dinner party in Georgetown.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes did a deal at a party once. Her friend Melissa Overmyer met a prospective buyer for the P Street house she owned with her husband, Dale, an architect, at one Georgetown soir\xc3\xa9e. Bubes was at another. \xe2\x80\x9cI start getting one text after another from Melissa, saying she has sold her house and that they are agreeing to all the terms on a cocktail napkin,\xe2\x80\x9d when in comes a photo of Melissa and the new buyer with said napkin, which read, \xe2\x80\x9c$2.9MM . . . No contingency, cash offer.\xe2\x80\x9d The following day, Dale Overmyer remembers, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy said, \xe2\x80\x98You are going to honor your cocktail napkin. A deal\xe2\x80\x99s a deal.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Bubes had the listing for the 8,200- square- foot \xe2\x80\x9cKennedy House,\xe2\x80\x9d where Jackie lived after JFK\xe2\x80\x99s assassination. Walk into the George Town Club with Bubes and she\xe2\x80\x99s helloing all over the room. Every July, she and her seven- person staff plant 1,600 American flags in Georgetown front yards, her business card attached. Every Halloween, her corner- lot Victorian turns into a haunted house with animatronic mummies and monsters, a creepy fortuneteller right out of a penny arcade, and ghostly noises emanating from the bushes. The whole neighborhood is there rubbernecking while Bubes, her ginger- blond hair poking out of an oversize witch\xe2\x80\x99s hat, mans a popcorn station or hands out candy bars. Bubes has occupied the 5,000- square- footer at 31st and Q for 18 years, only the second she\xe2\x80\x99s owned in the neighborhood. (She also has homes in Palm Beach and Rehoboth.) \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m a Taurus,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI get very settled.\xe2\x80\x9d But she\xe2\x80\x99ll know before you do when you\xe2\x80\x99re ready for a refresh and what might suit. \xe2\x80\x9cShe knows everybody in town and every house in town,\xe2\x80\x9d says Mary Louise Kelly , a novelist and NPR\xe2\x80\x99s national- security correspondent. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen one comes open, she\xe2\x80\x99ll run into someone she knows and say, \xe2\x80\x98Maybe you should think about moving.\xe2\x80\x99 Suddenly in a week, you\xe2\x80\x99re buying a house.\xe2\x80\x9d Kelly and her husband did. They were living in Italy temporarily, not really looking to leave their permanent residence on O Street, but suddenly a gray Victorian on Q with a white lattice portico and that rarest of finds in Georgetown\xe2\x80\x94 parking\xe2\x80\x94 appeared. They saw the house on a quick trip to DC, had no financing in place, made an offer. \xe2\x80\x9cMy husband, Nick, was on crutches and couldn\xe2\x80\x99t walk,\xe2\x80\x9d says Kelly. \xe2\x80\x9cHe was literally crawling up and down the stairs on all fours.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes had no trouble unloading the O Street property they left behind. She knew a couple on R who wanted to downsize. Bubes hails from Fredericksburg, Virginia, where her father, Clyde, owned an auto- parts store and her mother, Mabel, played piano in the Baptist church. One day, for my benefit, she calls her cousin Norman from her Champagne- colored 740- series BMW and puts him on speaker. \xe2\x80\x9cHello, doll baby,\xe2\x80\x9d he says in the family\xe2\x80\x99s Dukes of Hazzard accent that Bubes has worked hard to lose. \xe2\x80\x9cHow you doing with those crooks up in Washington?\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes graduated from East Carolina University with a degree in fine art. But \xe2\x80\x9cI was not going to be selling pots for a living,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. She moved to Washington hoping to work at the Smithsonian, but no positions were open, so she began selling designer clothing at Neiman Marcus. At 29, after a transfer to the in- house PR department, Bubes was fired, she says, for eating a piece of chocolate while working a party. From there, she got involved in neighborhood groups and met \xe2\x80\x9clayer upon layer of people.\xe2\x80\x9d By her mid- forties, which coincided with the market boom of the early 2000s, Bubes began to dominate DC. Her sales went from $55 million in 2002 to $100 million just three years later. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen everyone was selling $1- million- to- $2- million homes, Nancy sold $500,000- to- $600,000 houses and sold lots of them,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan Bubes, her husband of 28 years. \xe2\x80\x9cWhen the market moved to $3 million to $4 million, she was doing $1 million to $2 million. She did a great job of staying just under what everyone else in the market was doing.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes used to market herself on the front of Safeway grocery carts. Then Trish Yan , her assistant from 2002 to 2009, helped engineer a Don Draper\xe2\x80\x93 style rebranding that cast Bubes as the aspirational ideal through an ad that ran in local glossies. Lounging poolside in the ad were two women, one of whom looked like Bubes. \xe2\x80\x9cThe headline was DISCOVER A WASHINGTON WAY OF LIFE,\xe2\x80\x9d says Yan. Bubes had moved into a new league. \xe2\x80\x9cThe first time we filed a tax return, my mouth dropped,\xe2\x80\x9d Alan says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe is always out- earning me.\xe2\x80\x9d Alan was until recently CEO of Linens of the Week, a major supplier to the food- service industry. The couple met early in Nancy\xe2\x80\x99s career when she showed Alan\xe2\x80\x99s mother a property on Grace Street in Georgetown. Bubes m\xc3\xa8re didn\xe2\x80\x99t like the place. But she said to her son, \xe2\x80\x9cShow her your apartment and have her sell that!\xe2\x80\x9d After dating four years, Alan and Nancy eloped to Lake Tahoe. Close friend Maury Povich , the TV personality, whom Alan met when he was 16 and caddied for at Woodmont Country Club, was the only witness. Instead of a wedding album, the Bubeses have a video of Povich spoofing his hosting duties on A Current Affair . The couple is so close to Povich and his wife, Connie Chung , that the latter are godparents to one of the Bubeses\xe2\x80\x99 three children. The foursome take vacations together twice a year\xe2\x80\x94 the rare instance, Chung says, when Bubes can survive with no plans or schedule: \xe2\x80\x9cWe just become idiots and let our brains rest.\xe2\x80\x9d Otherwise, \xe2\x80\x9cNancy is relentless in her work.\xe2\x80\x9d She \xe2\x80\x9calways has to be busy,\xe2\x80\x9d says Alan. \xe2\x80\x9cIf she\xe2\x80\x99s on the phone, she\xe2\x80\x99s on her stationary bike or needlepointing belt after belt. She did sell a house when she was in labor with our first child.\xe2\x80\x9d Says Nancy: \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t have an assistant back then!\xe2\x80\x9d Watching Bubes in action is like watching one of those Hindu deities with all the arms. Take the splashy preview party she hosted at the Massachusetts Avenue Heights home of the late financier and venture capitalist Melvyn Estrin . Looking like the offspring of Versailles and the Parthenon, the mansion had two life- size sculptures of horses prancing on the front lawn, neoclassical statues all over the marble foyer, and carpets so pristine that everyone was given surgical slippers before being allowed upstairs, where Estrin\xe2\x80\x99s widow received guests while perched on a settee in the master suite\xe2\x80\x99s sitting room. Throughout the affair, Bubes stood under a skylight rotunda, somehow managing to balance a plate of hors d\xe2\x80\x99oeuvres, hug and kiss almost everyone who walked in\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cOkay, you\xe2\x80\x99re going to die\xe2\x80\x9d is her hello\xe2\x80\x94 and pass out swag bags. The house\xe2\x80\x99s sale price two weeks later: $12.25 million. \xe2\x80\x9cNancy is a \xe2\x80\x98periodic,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d explains Doug Komlenic, her business coach. \xe2\x80\x9cShe can engage me and you and engage in a multiple of other tasks and be present and in the moment for each one\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94 a true multitasker. Komlenic, who\xe2\x80\x99s based in San Diego, talks to Bubes twice a month. He discourages other clients from checking e- mail or reading their newsfeeds, but not Nancy. Of 83 clients, she\xe2\x80\x99s one of only two periodics\xe2\x80\x94 a human Lamborghini, prone to leaving those who can\xe2\x80\x99t keep up in the dust. \xe2\x80\x9cOur challenge from day one has been managing her team,\xe2\x80\x9d Komlenic says. \xe2\x80\x9cWe are looking for someone to be more of a leader so Nancy can delegate and then that person can delegate.\xe2\x80\x9d she used to market herself on safeway grocery carts. then things changed. Yan, who used to be that person, remembers doing juice cleanses with Bubes and being such intimates that they could finish each other\xe2\x80\x99s sentences. Not that working for Bubes was all sleepovers and hair braiding: \xe2\x80\x9cThe phone would ring at all hours or Nancy would knock on my door at 7 am and say, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, I\xe2\x80\x99m just walking by.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d While in Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s employ, Yan rented a house on 33rd Street that Bubes owned. The arrangement contributed to their eventual breakup, after Yan came to believe that Bubes had outright purchased a home for the family nanny. \xe2\x80\x9cIf I\xe2\x80\x99ve grown someone to $100 million and I\xe2\x80\x99m still renting?\xe2\x80\x9d Yan asks reflectively. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s a huge disconnect.\xe2\x80\x9d Says Bubes: \xe2\x80\x9cI feel badly that she felt this way, because we were very, very close.\xe2\x80\x9d Cara McClure , who\xe2\x80\x99s now a real- estate agent in Palm Beach, enjoyed the not- in- the- job- description duties Bubes assigned her during her 18 months with the firm, such as caddying for Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son. \xe2\x80\x9cI remember thinking, \xe2\x80\x98I get to have fun at this golf course and get a good workout at the same time!\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Less fun was the night McClure stayed late to help Bubes with her taxes. \xe2\x80\x9cBut Nancy brought me home- cooked salmon and sat there with me the whole time,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99ll be in the trenches with you.\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes takes on everyone as family, according to McClure. In fact, she did buy a place to house the nanny\xe2\x80\x94 but it was not a gift. She charged her rent, just as she did Yan. The home was close to Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s son\xe2\x80\x99s school, so the nanny could take over carpooling duties. Bubes was too afraid to chauffeur the boy herself, the result of a highway mishap seven years ago. She was driving back to DC from Fredericksburg and ran out of gas, right in the middle lane of I- 395. (A good Samaritan pulled over and walked across three lanes of traffic to rescue her.) A conflicted relationship with the automobile is yet another reason why Bubes has integrated so well into car- averse Georgetown. Also why she has a very good therapist. \xe2\x80\x9cShe came to one of my open houses,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says, cracking up. \xe2\x80\x9cWe started talking, and she says that she\xe2\x80\x99s a behavioral therapist who specializes in phobias and I said, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, my God, do I need you.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Yes, Bubes sold her shrink a house. A real- estate agent who\xe2\x80\x99s leery of driving seems like the ultimate paradox, but within the roughly two- square- mile province where Bubes reigns, it squares perfectly. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m not driving 30 minutes to show something in Virginia,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m running five blocks.\xe2\x80\x9d Running those blocks at Bubes\xe2\x80\x99s side is like trying to keep up with a mayor. She stops to talk to absolutely everyone, using first names and asking after the whole brood\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s all about dogs and kids,\xe2\x80\x9d she tells me\xe2\x80\x94 or inquiring about an upcoming fundraiser of which she herself is usually a chair, donor, or board member. Bubes belongs to Adas Israel, having converted from Protestantism to Judaism after she married. She also attends 7:30 Mass at Epiphany Catholic Church every morning. She has her bases covered. \xe2\x80\x9cI did that sale,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, pointing to the notorious Drath house, where in 2011 47- year- old Albrecht Muth murdered Viola Drath, his 91- year- old wife. There\xe2\x80\x99s a house on P Street that she\xe2\x80\x99s sold four times since 1997. The one- percenter buyers of today\xe2\x80\x99s Washington want clean lines, contemporary interiors, \xe2\x80\x9cbigger closets, bigger bathrooms, and en- suite master bathrooms with a double sink,\xe2\x80\x9d Bubes says. Also: \xe2\x80\x9cThey all want elevators.\xe2\x80\x9d She stages her clients\xe2\x80\x99 homes in the full spectrum of neutrals and advises them to paint the walls white. \xe2\x80\x9cTwenty years ago,\xe2\x80\x9d she says, \xe2\x80\x9cyou had more people who would . . . replicate the Victorian style\xe2\x80\x94 interiors with dark wood, heavy drapery.\xe2\x80\x9d Back then, she recalls fondly, \xe2\x80\x9cpeople paid extra for those sloping floors.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9c$2.9MM . . . No contingency, cash offer\xe2\x80\x9d: a deal done on a cocktail napkin. Bubes was at one Georgetown party; her seller and buyer wrote out the terms at another. Not that she\xe2\x80\x99s nostalgic. There\xe2\x80\x99s always a deep- pocketed developer (Douglas Jemal) or attorney (\xe2\x80\x9cDC Hammer\xe2\x80\x9d Mike Slocumb) among her past clients who might call up in search of a change of scenery. Her VIPs are one subject on which Bubes is remarkably tight- lipped. Asked about a rumor that she recently took a Middle Eastern royal house- hunting, she declines to comment. What about whispers of her having hung out at the Trump Hotel on Christmas Eve\xe2\x80\x94 and not just because she likes the lobby Ivanka Trump designed? Another no- comment. Bubes does credit the Trump White House for a recent upsurge in business. \xe2\x80\x9cIn my 32 years of being in real estate, this is the first time we\xe2\x80\x99ve had an administration come in that\xe2\x80\x99s buying and not renting,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. As of this writing, Bubes has ten listings totaling about $50 million. The Kennedy house is still among them. It\xe2\x80\x99s been reduced again, to $6.5 million. But owner Dolly Fox isn\xe2\x80\x99t worried. Bubes has sold property for her before. \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99ve dealt with a lot of realtors,\xe2\x80\x9d Fox says, \xe2\x80\x9cand it\xe2\x80\x99s always Nancy who comes in at the last minute and sells.\xe2\x80\x9d Photographs by Marquis Perkins.
